symptoms.symptom_name: Spontaneous burning, throbbing regional pain with allodynia (light touch provokes severe pain)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Spontaneous burning pain with allodynia out of proportion to the injury is a core sensory feature of CRPS (Budapest criteria).
DVT pain is typically a deep aching whole-limb heaviness, not superficial burning allodynia.

symptoms.symptom_name: Vasomotor instability — colour fluctuation dusky blue to red with livedo reticularis

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Vasomotor changes (skin colour asymmetry/fluctuation, livedo) are a defining sign category in the Budapest criteria.

symptoms.symptom_name: Affected hand cooler than the contralateral side (temperature asymmetry)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Temperature asymmetry is a CRPS vasomotor sign; a cold limb is characteristic of the sympathetically-mediated pattern.
Joint/tendon-sheath infection produces a warm, hot limb — a cold hand argues against active infection.
Cellulitis is warm and erythematous; a cool limb argues against it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Non-pitting oedema / swelling of the hand

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Oedema is a CRPS sudomotor/oedema sign category feature.
Limb swelling is non-specific and keeps DVT on the differential until other features discriminate.

symptoms.symptom_name: Onset after minor wrist trauma 4 weeks prior; strictly unilateral regional distribution
symptoms.onset: regional pain 4 weeks after a minor wrist sprain, acute flare over 12 hours

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: CRPS-I follows a noxious event/injury (often minor) with pain disproportionate to the inciting event.
A unilateral, post-traumatic regional distribution is inconsistent with a symmetrical systemic neuropathy.
Absence of fever/systemic illness lowers the probability of joint/tendon-sheath infection, though it does not exclude it.
Cellulitis is often accompanied by systemic features; afebrile status argues against significant infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: No spreading erythema, red streaking, pus, or a hot fluctuant joint (pertinent negative)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of advancing erythema/streaking is needed to clinically down-rank cellulitis.
A cool, non-erythematous hand argues against active joint/tendon-sheath infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain markedly worsened by cold/ice application

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Cold hypersensitivity and worsening of pain with cold exposure is characteristic of sympathetically-mediated CRPS and is a management pitfall (ice is harmful).

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain-driven autonomic response — nausea, presyncope-type dizziness, subjective tachycardia

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Nausea, lightheadedness and a racing heart are a non-specific autonomic response to severe pain, not an independent escalation trigger once infection and ischaemia are screened negative.

symptoms.symptom_name: Warmth, fluctuance, joint effusion or Kanavel signs on palpation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Palpation for warmth, fluctuance, effusion and Kanavel signs is the bedside means of excluding joint/tendon-sheath infection — unavailable via telehealth, so this remains a sealed clinician finding and a mandatory in-person safety-net.

symptoms.symptom_name: Inflammatory markers and joint aspirate (would help confirm/exclude infection)

investigation_details.expected_result: Normal or only mildly raised markers would be consistent with a sympathetic pain flare; markedly raised markers or a purulent aspirate would indicate infection — cannot be obtained via telehealth in real time.
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Without in-person assessment and markers, an early septic arthritis / flexor tenosynovitis cannot be definitively excluded.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Inflammatory markers and joint aspirate are the definitive means of excluding infection when clinical assessment is equivocal.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I)
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: No single feature is diagnostic, but burning allodynia + vasomotor colour fluctuation + temperature asymmetry + oedema + a preceding minor injury + cold-worsened pain together form a high-probability CRPS-I constellation that reframes the alarming presentation as a benign-cause pain flare rather than an emergency.

Afebrile plus a cool, non-erythematous, non-streaking hand collectively lowers the probability of joint/tendon-sheath infection and supports outpatient management with safety-netting rather than emergency escalation — but does not fully exclude infection without palpation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person hand examination (warmth, fluctuance, Kanavel signs, perfusion)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Bedside examination within 24–48 hours is the key step to confirm CRPS features and clinically exclude joint/tendon-sheath infection — the finding telehealth cannot obtain.
Support exclusion of infection when the clinical picture is equivocal; arrange with the in-person review.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Plain radiograph / three-phase bone scan of the hand
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Adjunct to support a CRPS diagnosis and assess for other bony pathology later in the pathway — not required acutely and not first-line in the telehealth encounter.

Not indicated once the burning allodynia, cold limb and sympathetic colour changes make CRPS the leading diagnosis and DVT is down-ranked — reserve only if isolated limb-swelling features predominate on in-person review.
